target.name: Palantir
target.founder: Peter Thiel, Alex Karp, Stephen Cohen, Joe Lonsdale, Nathan Gettings

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in AI & Software (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 1 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 17 same-sector peers exist in AI & Software, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

data.narrative:
Palantir is a public-stage AI & Software company with a current valuation exceeding $400B and pre-IPO total raised of $3B+. The peer universe in AI & Software comprises 17 same-sector comparables, though a narrow peer set meeting rigorous stage and valuation-tier alignment criteria is limited: 1 public comparable (PLTR itself) and 8 private peers including Waymo, Figure AI, Scale AI, Applied Intuition, and Skild AI.

The peer-median triangulation method is not applicable here. A filtered cohort of same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier peers yielded fewer than three datapoints, falling below the minimum threshold required for robust statistical inference. Broadening the filter to all 17 AI & Software peers would introduce heterogeneity across business models, revenue maturity, and capital efficiency that would render the median uninformative rather than instructive.

Given the constraint on comparable-company analysis, valuation support for Palantir at $400B+ cannot be derived from the triangulation methods available in this dataset. The company's scale, public-market status, and differentiated position within AI & Software create a cohort problem endemic to frontier-stage valuations. Determination of valuation defensibility would require additional methods—discounted cash flow modeling, precedent transaction analysis, or scenario-based revenue attribution—or a denser private-peer dataset with disclosed valuation and revenue metrics.
